title: India’s Healthcare Paradox and the defining Link Between Access and Outcomes
summary: One reason outcomes still lag is that India’s disease burden has shifted sharply toward chronic illness, which do not resolve with one visit. India has done the hard work of expanding access, the next phase is harder, but it is where lives will be saved. We...
thbAction: Position THB's pharma CRM and omnichannel HCP engagement workflows to support launch planning, KAM orchestration, and compliant follow-up across India and the Middle East.
contentHtml:
Over the last decade, India has made real progress on access. Insurance coverage has expanded, more hospitals are empanelled, digital health rails are coming up, and primary care is reaching deeper into communities. Yet in boardrooms and OPDs, we keep hearing the same argument - Care is getting efficient, how do we make it more effective?That gap is real. And it has less to do with whether a patient can enter the system, and more to do with what happens after they enter it. If India’s next leap is about outcomes, then the need is to fix the weak links that sit between the first consultation and long-term recovery.

Advt

Access has improved, but outcomes need more than entry

On paper, India’s access story is strong. As of 28 February 2026, the government reported 43.52 crore Ayushman cards and 36,229 empanelled hospitals under AB PM-JAY, including a large private hospital network. Digital health is scaling too, with 86 crore plus ABHA accounts and 90 crore plus health records linked as of February 2026.

This is meaningful progress. But outcomes are not a single hospital admission. Outcomes are a timeline. They depend on early diagnosis, correct clinical decisions, patient adherence, follow-up, and the ability to sustain care for months or years. When those links break, improved access does not automatically convert into better outcomes. So the solution cannot be limited to “more access.” The solution has to be “better continuity and better execution after access.”

India is treating more, but the disease mix has changed

One reason outcomes still lag is that India’s disease burden has shifted sharply toward chronic illness. Non-communicable diseases are now a dominant part of the load. The Ministry of Health’s NP-NCD operational guideline cites WHO’s India NCD profile that estimates NCDs account for about 63% of all deaths in the country.

Chronic diseases behave differently from acute infections. They do not resolve with one visit. Diabetes, hypertension, heart disease, kidney disease, COPD, and cancer need long-term management. Missed follow-ups, irregular medicines, and delayed tests silently worsen outcomes.

Advt

The practical fix here is to build chronic care pathways that are easy to follow, not just clinically correct. That means fewer “one-time prescriptions” and more structured plans with scheduled check-ins, monitoring milestones, and simple adherence support. If a patient is expected to return in 14 days, the system should make that return easier, not optional.

The real gap is continuity of care

In my view, the single biggest reason for lag is broken continuity. We still run too much healthcare as “episodes.” A patient comes in during a crisis, gets treated, and disappears until the next crisis.

Advt

This is where outcomes are lost. Not because doctors do not know what to do, but because the patient’s care pathway is not held together. They are often managing multiple prescriptions, multiple labs, multiple referrals, and multiple opinions without a clear coordinator.Digital records help, but they do not automatically create continuity. Continuity needs practical systems: follow-up schedules, reminders, care navigators, home monitoring for high-risk patients, and a clear plan that a family can actually follow.

Quality is uneven, and early diagnosis still happens late

The second hard truth is variation in quality. India has world-class clinical talent, but patient outcomes vary widely based on where a person first enters care.

Many patients still reach specialist care late. Screening is inconsistent. Early warning symptoms are treated, but root causes are not investigated in time. Referrals happen, but there is leakage in between. By the time a patient reaches the right specialist or the right facility, the disease is often more advanced and harder to treat.This is why access alone cannot solve outcomes. A patient needs access to timely and correct care, not just any care.

Workforce availability is not only a numbers problem

India often cites its doctor-to-population ratio, and officially, the Ministry of Health has stated that India’s doctor-population ratio is 1:811, better than the WHO standard of 1:1000, along with 39.40 lakh nursing personnel present in the country.But outcomes are affected by distribution, skill mix, and time per patient. The same press note acknowledges the scale of registered doctors and nurses, yet what patients experience on the ground can still be thin staffing, especially outside large urban centres. When nurses are stretched, discharge education becomes rushed. When follow-up capacity is low, early deterioration is missed. When primary care is overloaded, chronic diseases stay uncontrolled until complications appear.In short, workforce matters not just in headcount, but in how care is delivered day to day.

Affordability still interrupts care

Yes, financial protection has improved. Out-of-pocket expenditure has reduced over time, but it is still a major behavioural driver. A PIB release in March 2025 stated that out-of-pocket expenditure as a share of total health expenditure declined to 39.4% in 2021–22 (from 62.6% in 2014–15).Even at 39.4%, the effect is huge. Families delay follow-up tests, skip physiotherapy, ration medicines, and avoid repeat visits once the immediate crisis has passed. That directly affects outcomes, especially for chronic conditions and post-surgical recovery. Coverage helps people start treatment. But outcomes improve when people can finish the journey.

What must change now

If we want outcomes to catch up with access, we need to treat continuity as core infrastructure. Three shifts matter for this most. Stronger early detection and structured pathways for chronic disease, so we catch problems before complications. Consistent quality at the first point of care, because the first diagnosis often decides the whole trajectory. And, care beyond hospital walls. Post-discharge monitoring, rehabilitation, nursing support, and home-based follow-up for high-risk patients are not “add-ons.” They are outcome tools.Home healthcare is especially important for elderly patients, post-surgical recovery, mothers and newborns, chronic disease patients, and anyone with mobility or access constraints. When nursing, physiotherapy, vitals monitoring, and follow-up checks shift closer to the home, complications are caught earlier and recovery becomes safer.India has done the hard work of expanding access. The next phase is harder, but it is where lives will be saved. We have to make care consistent, continuous, and easier to sustain. That is how access turns into outcomes.The article is written by Vishal Lathwal, CEO, Apollo Home Healthcare, Apollo Hospitals.(DISCLAIMER: The views expressed are solely of the author and ETHealthworld.com does not necessarily subscribe to it.
